items.q: Is the proton a fundamental particle?
items.correct: No — it is a baryon made of two up quarks and one down quark.
items.misconception: Yes — protons cannot be divided any further.
items.random_wrong: No — protons are made of three electrons.
items.why: GCSE treats protons as basic building blocks, so students assume they are elementary rather than composite.

items.q: In beta-minus decay, where does the emitted electron come from?
items.correct: It is created when a neutron changes into a proton, together with an antineutrino.
items.misconception: It is an orbital electron ejected from its shell.
items.random_wrong: It was stored inside the nucleus all along.
items.why: Students assume the beta particle must already exist somewhere in the atom; it is created in the weak interaction.

items.q: Does brighter (more intense) light give each photoelectron more kinetic energy?
items.correct: No — intensity increases the number emitted; only a higher frequency raises the maximum kinetic energy.
items.misconception: Yes — brighter light means more energetic electrons.
items.random_wrong: Yes — bright light heats the electrons until they boil off.
items.why: Wave thinking says energy accumulates with intensity, but each electron is freed by a single photon whose energy is hf.

items.q: The quark composition of a neutron is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: udd. “uss” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which of these is a lepton?
items.why: It's The electron. “The proton” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: When a particle meets its antiparticle, they:
items.why: Correct answer: Annihilate, producing photons.. “Form a stable atom.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In the photoelectric effect, no electrons are emitted below the threshold frequency because:
items.correct: Each photon carries too little energy to overcome the work function.
items.misconception: Electrons are repelled by low-frequency light.
items.why: It's Each photon carries too little energy to overcome the work function.. “Electrons are repelled by low-frequency light.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A photon has frequency 5×10¹⁴ Hz and h ≈ 6.6×10⁻³⁴ J s. What is its energy, in units of 10⁻¹⁹ J?
items.why: Exact answer: 3.3 ×10⁻¹⁹ J. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
